page: Vultr vbm-6c-32gb-amd is a bare metal server belonging to the Bare Metal NVMe family. It features an AMD EPYC 4245P processor built on the x86_64 architecture, operating at a clock speed of 3.8 GHz. The hardware configuration includes 6 physical cores and 12 vCPUs with 2 threads per core, paired with 32.0 GB of system memory, yielding 2.67 GB of RAM per core. Local storage is provided by a 1920 GB NVMe SSD, and the server includes one complimentary public IPv4 address. No GPU accelerators are present on this instance.

Benchmark data indicates strong single-threaded performance. The server achieves top-tier status in stress-ng single-core CPU performance, single-threaded gzip compression, and single-threaded bzip3 decompression. The PassMark memory score also ranks in the top tier. However, a notable performance tradeoff is observed in memory write bandwidth for large uncached blocks, which falls into the bottom 25%. Multi-core CPU performance, database operations, and static web serving throughput align with average mid-tier benchmarks. For machine learning workloads, the system delivers top-tier text generation speeds for small 135M parameter models, though performance drops to average levels when processing medium 7B parameter models.

From a qualitative cost-efficiency perspective, this bare metal server offers high single-core compute density and substantial local NVMe storage relative to its resource profile. It is suited for workloads that benefit from high clock speeds and dedicated hardware without virtualization overhead. Appropriate workloads include single-threaded data processing, static web serving, database operations, and lightweight language model inference.
description: Vultr vbm-6c-32gb-amd is an x86_64 bare metal server powered by an AMD EPYC 4245P processor running at 3.8 GHz. It features 6 physical cores, 12 vCPUs, 32.0 GB of RAM, and a 1920 GB NVMe SSD. Benchmark results highlight top-tier single-core CPU performance, single-threaded compression, and PassMark memory scores. Conversely, large-block memory write bandwidth represents a performance tradeoff, ranking in the bottom 25%. Multi-core performance, database operations, and static web serving throughput remain in the average tier. This server is suited for workloads requiring high clock speeds and dedicated local storage, such as single-threaded processing, database hosting, static web serving, and small-scale LLM inference.
